title: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
oneLiner: Common median nerve entrapment at wrist; presents with hand paraesthesiae; early treatment prevents permanent thenar muscle wasting.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute forearm compartment syndrome** — tense swelling, pain out of proportion, pain on passive finger extension, pulselessness (late sign). *Rule out before treating CTS.*
- **Cervical cord compression** (e.g., acute disc prolapse, tumour) — bilateral hand symptoms, hyperreflexia, clonus, gait disturbance, urinary retention. *Immediate MRI spine + neurosurgical referral.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Nocturnal paraesthesiae (classically wakes patient, shaking hand relieves)
  - Numbness/tingling in thumb, index, middle, and radial ring finger
  - Clumsiness, dropping objects, loss of fine motor control
  - Pain radiating proximally up forearm (rarely to shoulder)
  - Symptoms worse with wrist flexion (driving, phone use)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Phalen's test** (flexed wrist for 60 seconds) — reproduces symptoms (sensitive)
  - **Tinel's sign** (tapping over carpal tunnel) — electric shock sensation (less sensitive)
  - **Thenar wasting** (abductor pollicis brevis) — late/significant
  - **Sensory loss** in median nerve distribution (volar aspect first 3.5 digits)
  - **Motor deficit** — weakness of thumb abduction (ask patient to lift thumb perpendicular to palm against resistance)
  - **Bilateral** in ~50% of cases; consider systemic cause if both hands affected
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Cervical radiculopathy (C6/C7)** — neck pain, dermatomal pattern (C6: thumb; C7: middle finger), positive Spurling's test, decreased reflexes (biceps, brachioradialis)
- **Peripheral neuropathy (e.g., diabetes, hypothyroidism)** — symmetric glove-and-stocking distribution, absent ankle jerks, bilateral
- **De Quervain's tenosynovitis** — pain over radial styloid, positive Finkelstein test, no sensory loss
- **Raynaud's phenomenon** — cold-induced colour changes, no motor signs
- **Pronator teres syndrome** — forearm tenderness, symptom reproduced by pronation against resistance, normal on electrodiagnostics
- **Thoracic outlet syndrome** — worse with arm elevation, ulnar distribution, associated with vascular symptoms
- **Splitter:** *Phalen's test positive + night waking = CTS*; *neck pain + radicular pattern = cervical radiculopathy*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Phalen's test, Tinel's sign, thenar muscle strength
  - Sensory testing with monofilament or cotton wisp
  - Two-point discrimination (normal < 6 mm; > 6 mm suggests advanced compression)
  - Grip strength (dynamometer if available)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, ESR/CRP (if inflammatory arthritis suspected)
  - Thyroid function, HbA1c, random glucose (to exclude diabetes/hypothyroidism)
  - Urate if gout considered
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** None needed for typical CTS
  - **Second-line:** Wrist ultrasound if atypical (e.g., ganglion, tenosynovitis, mass) or to guide injection
  - **MRI wrist** if structural lesion suspected but rarely needed
- **Special tests:**
  - **Nerve conduction studies (NCS)** — gold-standard; confirms diagnosis if ambiguous or before surgery; measures motor/sensory distal latencies, amplitude, conduction velocity
    - Key threshold: median sensory latency > 3.5 ms (prolonged) or difference from ulnar > 0.5 ms
  - **Electromyography (EMG)** — assesses denervation in thenar muscles (late finding)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **CTS-6 symptoms-compatible** (≥ 3 of: nocturnal paraesthesia, thenar wasting, positive Phalen, positive Tinel, sensory loss, motor deficit)
  - **Clinical diagnosis** is sufficient in typical cases without NCS if symptoms are classic and exam supportive
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age (CTS can be gestational; influences management — avoid surgery during pregnancy)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Reassurance; condition rarely an emergency
  - If acute severe compression (e.g., trauma, haematoma, infection) — urgent orthopaedic/hand surgery referral for possible decompression
  - Remove any wrist splints/rings that may worsen compression
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Usually managed outpatient; admission only for post-operative care after release
  - For post-op: elevate hand, ice packs, simple analgesia (paracetamol), early active finger movements
  - If admitted for other reasons — generic wrist splint at night, NSAIDs if tolerated
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Conservative:**
    - Night wrist splint (neutral position) for 6–12 weeks
    - Activity modification (avoid sustained flexion/extension)
    - NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen 500 mg po BD for 2–4 weeks — [check local policy])
    - Corticosteroid injection (see drugs below)
  - **Surgical referral if:**
    - Failure of conservative therapy after 3–6 months
    - Severe sensory loss or motor deficit (thenar wasting)
    - Electrodiagnostically moderate-to-severe
    - Bilateral CTS with significant functional impairment
  - **Surgery:** Carpal tunnel release (open or endoscopic) — day-case, local anaesthetic possible
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Not typically applicable; if post-op: regular paracetamol ± ibuprofen, wound review, DVT prophylaxis not needed for day-case, NBM pre-op only.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **NSAIDs** (oral): Ibuprofen 400–600 mg TDS max 28 days; or naproxen 500 mg BD after food [check local policy]
  - **Corticosteroid injection:**
    - Methylprednisolone acetate 40 mg (1 mL) mixed with lidocaine 1% (0.5–1 mL) injected into carpal tunnel (ulnar to palmaris longus tendon)
    - Max 3 injections per year; >2 may increase nerve damage risk
    - Alternative: triamcinolone 20 mg (0.5 mL) [check local policy]
  - **Night splints** — no drugs, but document instructed use
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Gabapentin** or **amitriptyline** for neuropathic pain (second-line; specialist use only)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: active peptic ulcer, severe renal impairment, asthma (caution), anticoagulation
  - Corticosteroid injection: local infection, allergy to anaesthetic, coagulopathy
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs avoid if eGFR <30 or decompensated liver disease
  - Corticosteroid injection: no systemic adjustment needed
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Corticosteroid injection — injecting directly into median nerve**: always aspirate before injecting; if severe paresthesia, withdraw immediately; may cause permanent nerve injury
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Post-injection flare (self-limiting)
  - Haematoma (especially if anticoagulated)
  - Acute median nerve compression from haematoma (rare) → urgent decompression
  - Infection (cellulitis, abscess) after injection/surgery
- **Chronic:**
  - Thenar muscle atrophy → irreversible weakness
  - Recurrence after injection (30–50% at 1 year)
  - Surgical complications: pillar pain (palmar scar tenderness), inadequate release, scar hypersensitivity, CRPS (complex regional pain syndrome)
  - Bilateral CTS: may indicate underlying systemic disease (hypothyroidism, diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, acromegaly, amyloidosis)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Recovery:** 70–90% improvement with surgery; returns to work in 2–6 weeks (sedentary) or 4–8 weeks (manual)
- **Persistence:** Up to 10% have residual symptoms (older age, severe denervation, diabetes)
- **Mortality:** Zero directly; unrecognised severe CTS can cause permanent hand disability
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check thenar bulk** — wasting is a late sign and indication for urgent referral (motor denervation is partially reversible)
- **Document Phalen's and Tinel's** in notes; baseline sensory/motor exam guides treatment response
- **Bilateral symptoms + thenar wasting → check pregnancy status, thyroid, and HbA1c** — treat underlying condition first
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don't rely solely on negative NCS to rule out CTS** — ~10–15% of clinical CTS have normal studies; treat based on symptoms
- **Never inject corticosteroid through an infected wrist** — can cause septic arthritis or severe tenosynovitis
- **Don't delay surgical referral once thenar wasting appears** — wait and see risks permanent weakness
- **Avoid prescribing strong opioids** — ineffective for neuropathic compression
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Sudden severe pain + swelling + cyanosis (rule out compartment syndrome)
  - Acute motor loss (thumb cannot lift off palm) — urgent surgical review
  - Signs of infection after injection/surgery: erythema, warmth, fever, purulent discharge
- **Follow-up:**
  - Conservative: review in 6 weeks; if no improvement → consider injection or NCS
  - Post-injection: if symptoms recur >3 months, consider repeat injection (max 3) or surgical referral
  - Post-surgery: wound review in 2 weeks, occupational therapy referral for severe weakness
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Registrar / Consultant) if:**
  - Suspected acute compartment syndrome (NEWS2 >3, tense forearm, severe pain on passive stretch)
  - Acute rapid motor loss (thenar paralysis) within hours
  - Suspected cervical cord compression (upper motor neuron signs, gait difficulty)
- **ICU / crash team:** Not indicated for CTS unless complication of anaesthetic or anaphylaxis from injection (rare)

rawMarkdown:
## Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
**One-liner:** Common median nerve entrapment at wrist; presents with hand paraesthesiae; early treatment prevents permanent thenar muscle wasting.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute forearm compartment syndrome** — tense swelling, pain out of proportion, pain on passive finger extension, pulselessness (late sign). *Rule out before treating CTS.*
- **Cervical cord compression** (e.g., acute disc prolapse, tumour) — bilateral hand symptoms, hyperreflexia, clonus, gait disturbance, urinary retention. *Immediate MRI spine + neurosurgical referral.*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Nocturnal paraesthesiae (classically wakes patient, shaking hand relieves)
  - Numbness/tingling in thumb, index, middle, and radial ring finger
  - Clumsiness, dropping objects, loss of fine motor control
  - Pain radiating proximally up forearm (rarely to shoulder)
  - Symptoms worse with wrist flexion (driving, phone use)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Phalen's test** (flexed wrist for 60 seconds) — reproduces symptoms (sensitive)
  - **Tinel's sign** (tapping over carpal tunnel) — electric shock sensation (less sensitive)
  - **Thenar wasting** (abductor pollicis brevis) — late/significant
  - **Sensory loss** in median nerve distribution (volar aspect first 3.5 digits)
  - **Motor deficit** — weakness of thumb abduction (ask patient to lift thumb perpendicular to palm against resistance)
  - **Bilateral** in ~50% of cases; consider systemic cause if both hands affected

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Cervical radiculopathy (C6/C7)** — neck pain, dermatomal pattern (C6: thumb; C7: middle finger), positive Spurling's test, decreased reflexes (biceps, brachioradialis)
- **Peripheral neuropathy (e.g., diabetes, hypothyroidism)** — symmetric glove-and-stocking distribution, absent ankle jerks, bilateral
- **De Quervain's tenosynovitis** — pain over radial styloid, positive Finkelstein test, no sensory loss
- **Raynaud's phenomenon** — cold-induced colour changes, no motor signs
- **Pronator teres syndrome** — forearm tenderness, symptom reproduced by pronation against resistance, normal on electrodiagnostics
- **Thoracic outlet syndrome** — worse with arm elevation, ulnar distribution, associated with vascular symptoms
- **Splitter:** *Phalen's test positive + night waking = CTS*; *neck pain + radicular pattern = cervical radiculopathy*

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Phalen's test, Tinel's sign, thenar muscle strength
  - Sensory testing with monofilament or cotton wisp
  - Two-point discrimination (normal < 6 mm; > 6 mm suggests advanced compression)
  - Grip strength (dynamometer if available)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, ESR/CRP (if inflammatory arthritis suspected)
  - Thyroid function, HbA1c, random glucose (to exclude diabetes/hypothyroidism)
  - Urate if gout considered
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** None needed for typical CTS
  - **Second-line:** Wrist ultrasound if atypical (e.g., ganglion, tenosynovitis, mass) or to guide injection
  - **MRI wrist** if structural lesion suspected but rarely needed
- **Special tests:**
  - **Nerve conduction studies (NCS)** — gold-standard; confirms diagnosis if ambiguous or before surgery; measures motor/sensory distal latencies, amplitude, conduction velocity
    - Key threshold: median sensory latency > 3.5 ms (prolonged) or difference from ulnar > 0.5 ms
  - **Electromyography (EMG)** — assesses denervation in thenar muscles (late finding)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **CTS-6 symptoms-compatible** (≥ 3 of: nocturnal paraesthesia, thenar wasting, positive Phalen, positive Tinel, sensory loss, motor deficit)
  - **Clinical diagnosis** is sufficient in typical cases without NCS if symptoms are classic and exam supportive
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age (CTS can be gestational; influences management — avoid surgery during pregnancy)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Reassurance; condition rarely an emergency
  - If acute severe compression (e.g., trauma, haematoma, infection) — urgent orthopaedic/hand surgery referral for possible decompression
  - Remove any wrist splints/rings that may worsen compression
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Usually managed outpatient; admission only for post-operative care after release
  - For post-op: elevate hand, ice packs, simple analgesia (paracetamol), early active finger movements
  - If admitted for other reasons — generic wrist splint at night, NSAIDs if tolerated
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Conservative:**
    - Night wrist splint (neutral position) for 6–12 weeks
    - Activity modification (avoid sustained flexion/extension)
    - NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen 500 mg po BD for 2–4 weeks — [check local policy])
    - Corticosteroid injection (see drugs below)
  - **Surgical referral if:**
    - Failure of conservative therapy after 3–6 months
    - Severe sensory loss or motor deficit (thenar wasting)
    - Electrodiagnostically moderate-to-severe
    - Bilateral CTS with significant functional impairment
  - **Surgery:** Carpal tunnel release (open or endoscopic) — day-case, local anaesthetic possible
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Not typically applicable; if post-op: regular paracetamol ± ibuprofen, wound review, DVT prophylaxis not needed for day-case, NBM pre-op only.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **NSAIDs** (oral): Ibuprofen 400–600 mg TDS max 28 days; or naproxen 500 mg BD after food [check local policy]
  - **Corticosteroid injection:**
    - Methylprednisolone acetate 40 mg (1 mL) mixed with lidocaine 1% (0.5–1 mL) injected into carpal tunnel (ulnar to palmaris longus tendon)
    - Max 3 injections per year; >2 may increase nerve damage risk
    - Alternative: triamcinolone 20 mg (0.5 mL) [check local policy]
  - **Night splints** — no drugs, but document instructed use
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Gabapentin** or **amitriptyline** for neuropathic pain (second-line; specialist use only)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: active peptic ulcer, severe renal impairment, asthma (caution), anticoagulation
  - Corticosteroid injection: local infection, allergy to anaesthetic, coagulopathy
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs avoid if eGFR <30 or decompensated liver disease
  - Corticosteroid injection: no systemic adjustment needed
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Corticosteroid injection — injecting directly into median nerve**: always aspirate before injecting; if severe paresthesia, withdraw immediately; may cause permanent nerve injury

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Post-injection flare (self-limiting)
  - Haematoma (especially if anticoagulated)
  - Acute median nerve compression from haematoma (rare) → urgent decompression
  - Infection (cellulitis, abscess) after injection/surgery
- **Chronic:**
  - Thenar muscle atrophy → irreversible weakness
  - Recurrence after injection (30–50% at 1 year)
  - Surgical complications: pillar pain (palmar scar tenderness), inadequate release, scar hypersensitivity, CRPS (complex regional pain syndrome)
  - Bilateral CTS: may indicate underlying systemic disease (hypothyroidism, diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, acromegaly, amyloidosis)

### Prognosis
- **Recovery:** 70–90% improvement with surgery; returns to work in 2–6 weeks (sedentary) or 4–8 weeks (manual)
- **Persistence:** Up to 10% have residual symptoms (older age, severe denervation, diabetes)
- **Mortality:** Zero directly; unrecognised severe CTS can cause permanent hand disability

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check thenar bulk** — wasting is a late sign and indication for urgent referral (motor denervation is partially reversible)
- **Document Phalen's and Tinel's** in notes; baseline sensory/motor exam guides treatment response
- **Bilateral symptoms + thenar wasting → check pregnancy status, thyroid, and HbA1c** — treat underlying condition first

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don't rely solely on negative NCS to rule out CTS** — ~10–15% of clinical CTS have normal studies; treat based on symptoms
- **Never inject corticosteroid through an infected wrist** — can cause septic arthritis or severe tenosynovitis
- **Don't delay surgical referral once thenar wasting appears** — wait and see risks permanent weakness
- **Avoid prescribing strong opioids** — ineffective for neuropathic compression

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Sudden severe pain + swelling + cyanosis (rule out compartment syndrome)
  - Acute motor loss (thumb cannot lift off palm) — urgent surgical review
  - Signs of infection after injection/surgery: erythema, warmth, fever, purulent discharge
- **Follow-up:**
  - Conservative: review in 6 weeks; if no improvement → consider injection or NCS
  - Post-injection: if symptoms recur >3 months, consider repeat injection (max 3) or surgical referral
  - Post-surgery: wound review in 2 weeks, occupational therapy referral for severe weakness

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (Registrar / Consultant) if:**
  - Suspected acute compartment syndrome (NEWS2 >3, tense forearm, severe pain on passive stretch)
  - Acute rapid motor loss (thenar paralysis) within hours
  - Suspected cervical cord compression (upper motor neuron signs, gait difficulty)
- **ICU / crash team:** Not indicated for CTS unless complication of anaesthetic or anaphylaxis from injection (rare)
